reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about the find_next_best_node() function, which picks the next-closest node for a fallback list from the full N_MEMORY set. The author refactored it into find_next_best_node_in(), which takes an explicit candidates nodemask, enabling building fallback lists with non-N_MEMORY candidates. No functional change is made, as every caller still selects from N_MEMORY.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about the complexity of build_zonelists() by extracting per-node fallback-list construction into a new function, build_node_zonelist(), which builds zonelists from candidate nodemasks instead of just the default N_MEMORY node state list. This change has no functional impact and is intended to simplify the code.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about the lack of explicit alloc_flags in bulk and folio allocation functions, explaining that they will add __alloc_pages_bulk_noprof() and modify existing functions to take an alloc_flags parameter, matching the behavior of __alloc_pages_noprof(). The changes are non-functional and only affect internal flag-carrying primitives.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about mutual exclusivity between N_MEMORY and N_MEMORY_PRIVATE states by explaining that they are mutually exclusive, adding checks to ensure this in node_private_register(), and clarifying the behavior of node_state() and helpers when CONFIG_NUMA is not set. A fix is planned for v2.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about unintentional allocations reaching N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes by making these nodes invisible to ordinary allocation and adding explicit private-node alloc() functions, but noted that private-node allocations can still fall back to non-private nodes.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern that cpuset operations should not partition private nodes via cpuset.mem and instead treat them as globally accessible resources. The author explained that isolating by cpuset offers no functionality and creates issues on rebind when a cpuset loses access to a node, generating migrations that may not be supported. They also stated that silent background operations on these nodes due to cpuset.mem membership changing should be avoided. A fix is planned for the next version.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about migrating private node memory without coordination by changing the hotunplug attempt to fail instead of spinning forever on a failed migration attempt, and requiring the owner to drain the folios safely before allowing hotunplug to proceed.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about private nodes being moved by mempolicy-driven migration, explaining that they are already kept out of policy nodemasks and adding a filter to skip private-node folios during migration. A fix is planned for the next version.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing a concern about userland driven migration requests for private node memory, which should be disallowed. The author has modified the mm/migrate.c file to use the same filter locations as zone_device folios to achieve this.

reviews.author: Omar Sandoval
reviews.summary: Reviewer Omar Sandoval noted that kcore.c may not require explicit handling in this series, suggesting it could be merged without additional changes to kcore.c.
reviews.raw_body:
I'm sure this is the least of your concern with this series, but FWIW:

Acked-by: Omar Sandoval <osandov@osandov.com>

If everything goes well, kcore.c is quiet enough that this can probably
just be merged through whatever tree takes the rest of this work.

2026-07-20.patch_summary: This patch introduces the concept of 'Private Memory Nodes', which are isolated by default and can be opted into specific services such as page allocation and OOM killing. The goal is to flip the current dynamic where NUMA-hosted memory is accessible by default, and instead isolate it and then opt-in to specific services that the device says is safe. This allows for more secure and efficient use of accelerators, special memory devices, network devices, and other types of memory. The patch reduces complexity and introduces a dedicated zonelist for private nodes, making it easier to manage and control memory access.

reviews.author: Balbir Singh
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not provide a clear explanation of how the new capabilities are being used, and requested additional documentation to clarify this.

The goal is to flip the current dynamic where NUMA-hosted memory is accessible by default, and instead isolate it and then opt-in to specific services that the device says is safe. This allows for more secure and efficient use of accelerators, special memory devices, network devices, and other types of memory.
